response: Supervised learning trains models on labeled data where each input has a known output. The model learns to map inputs to outputs by minimizing the difference between predictions and true labels. Common tasks include classification (predicting categories) and regression (predicting continuous values). Examples include spam detection and house price prediction.

context: <user> How do transformers handle long sequences?
response: Transformers handle long sequences using several techniques. The self-attention mechanism has quadratic complexity O(n^2) with sequence length, which is the main bottleneck. Solutions include sparse attention (attending to only a subset of positions), sliding window attention, and hierarchical approaches. Positional encodings like RoPE help the model understand token positions without recurrent structure.

PyTorch's autograd system records operations on tensors to build a directed acyclic graph (DAG) of computations. Each tensor has a requires_grad flag. When you perform operations, autograd records the gradient function. Calling backward() traverses the graph backward from the loss, computing gradients using the chain rule and accumulating them in each tensor's grad attribute. The graph is dynamic — rebuilt on every forward pass, which is why PyTorch is research-friendly.
